channel.title: Latest alerts from washingtonpost.com
channel.description: An unofficial feed created by Derek Willis.

item.title: Judge temporarily blocks Justice Department’s $1.8 billion payout fund
item.description: A federal judge in Virginia said the Trump administration may not transfer the money or process claims until at least June 12.

item.title: Wager your points and finish off the week with today’s On the Record
item.description: Take today’s news quiz and complete your point total for the week.

item.title: Five killed, dozens injured in crash involving bus and six other vehicles on Interstate 95 in Stafford County, Virginia
item.description: The crash occurred around 2:35 a.m. where traffic was slowing ahead of a work zone, according to Virginia State Police.

item.title: A court in Kenya blocks U.S. plan to quarantine Americans exposed to Ebola at makeshift hospital
item.description:
The court, citing a threat to life, issued its ruling on the day U.S. officials said the facility would begin operating.
It has capacity for up to 50 patients potentially exposed during the growing Ebola outbreak, which is centered in the Democratic Republic of Congo.

item.title: Iran talks. Trump stocks. 2028 contenders. Rocket explosion. GLP-1 drugs. Spelling Bee. WWII mystery. Catch up now.
item.description: Catch up in minutes with these 7 stories.

item.title: Blue Origin rocket explodes on launchpad during testing ahead of satellite mission
item.description: “We experienced an anomaly during today’s hotfire test,” the company said on social media, adding that all personnel had been accounted for. Blue Origin was preparing to send the reusable rocket to space for a mission delivering satellites into low Earth orbit.

item.title: Shrey Parikh, a 14-year-old from California, is the winner of the 2026 Scripps National Spelling Bee
item.description: Judges determined that Shrey spelled 32 words correctly during the spell-off, beating 12-year-old Ishaan Gupta’s 25 words.

item.title: The finals of the Scripps National Spelling Bee are underway with nine finalists. Follow our coverage.
item.description: The winner will take home a slew of prizes, including $50,000, a commemorative medal, the Scripps Cup, as well as $2,500 and a reference library from Merriam-Webster.

item.title: Dell inks $9.7 billion Pentagon contract after Trump acquires stock, praises company
item.description: Ethics experts say the president’s recent investments in the tech company create the appearance of a conflict of interest. Trump has repeatedly praised the company at public events, and government disclosure forms show that on Feb. 10 his portfolio acquired stock in Dell Technologies valued between $1 million and $5 million.

item.title: Ozempic may be reshaping the brain, scientists say
item.description: GLP-1 drugs may be rewiring circuits involved not only in appetite but in emotion, desire and beyond.

item.title: Trump has devastated Iran’s regime. Don’t give it a lifeline now.
item.description: Trump has devastated Iran’s regime. Don’t give it a lifeline now.

item.title: DOJ probes Democratic-allied nonprofit that helped fund columnist E. Jean Carroll’s lawsuit against Trump
item.description: The investigation centers on statements Carroll made during a deposition in her civil lawsuit against President Donald Trump, in which a jury found that he had sexually assaulted and defamed her.

item.title: As data centers boom, Virginians breathe the exhaust of 10,000 diesel generators
item.description: Pollution from generators at data centers could cause respiratory symptoms and deaths in the region, an analysis for The Washington Post found.

item.title: As data centers boom, Virginians breathe the exhaust of 10,000 diesel generators
item.description: Pollution from generators at data centers could cause respiratory symptoms and deaths in the region, an analysis for The Washington Post found.

item.title: Trump’s approval plunges among his White working-class base
item.description: In a striking shift, White voters without college degrees who voted to reelect Trump by a huge margin are now net-negative on his job approval.

item.title: Supreme Court sides with man on death row, effectively vacating his conviction over potential racial bias in jury selection
item.description: In a 5-4 vote, the court found Terry Pitchford should have had the opportunity to challenge the dismissal of four Black potential jurors in Mississippi.

item.title: A drug-fueled spectacle in Vegas shows humans are tough to beat
item.description: The Enhanced Games promised a revolution. It fizzled.

item.title: Big news in Texas in today’s On the Record
item.description: Big news in Texas in today’s On the Record

item.title: Tehran fires ballistic missile at U.S. air base in Kuwait in retaliation for bombing of Iranian launch site
item.description: The overnight strikes tested the countries’ fragile truce and their ongoing negotiations over a broader peace deal.

item.title: Trump appointees push $250 bill with his portrait
item.description: The director of the Bureau of Engraving and Printing who resisted the effort was reassigned last month. “The buck stopped here,” she wrote in her goodbye email.

item.title: Iran strike. Trump disapproval. Green cards. Gold bars. Mortgage rates. Blood sugar. Relationship secrets. Catch up now.
item.description: Catch up in minutes with these 7 stories.

item.title: Pregnant woman from Ghana detained for days with 4-year-old son at Dulles Airport, ACLU says
item.description: The pair arrived in the U.S. on a tourist visa to seek medical treatment, according to their lawyers, who allege that the mother and child have been confined to a windowless room for more than a week.

item.title: Did Trump pick the right blue for the Reflecting Pool? We asked a pool guy.
item.description: Old Glory Blue? American Flag Blue? Let’s reflect on all the shades, while a federal judge mulls “aesthetic injury” in the president’s latest decorating flourish.

item.title: Harvard vs. Trump is the result of a massive error
item.description: Harvard vs. Trump is the result of a massive error

item.title: We asked 1,200 Americans what they dislike about Democrats and Republicans
item.description: The wide-ranging criticisms — that the parties are corrupt, subservient, “wishy washy” or unable to handle the president — signal major challenges for both sides.

item.title: House Democrats announce bill to block construction of Trump’s arch
item.description: The legislation would explicitly bar new construction on the small island on the edge of Washington where President Donald Trump seeks to put the arch. The bill faces long odds in Congress, where Republican leaders have hesitated to publicly oppose Trump’s construction projects.

item.title: Trump administration begins making new requests of green-card applicants
item.description: U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services has started asking some applicants why they did not leave the United States and return home to apply for a green card, according to two lawyers with direct knowledge of the communications.

item.title: Months after feud with Bari Weiss, Sharyn Alfonsi is out at ‘60 Minutes’
item.description: The veteran CBS News correspondent slammed her boss after a segment on migrants detained at a prison in El Salvador was pulled from a December broadcast.

item.title: Friends make snarky comments about my wealth. Hax readers give advice.
item.description: Is a mid-40s letter writer, getting snark from friends about wealth, “supposed to be embarrassed” about early career success?

item.title: U.S. to send Americans exposed to Ebola to makeshift hospital in Kenya
item.description: Trump officials have instructed the U.S. military to stand up a quarantine facility in central Kenya within a week, according to two people familiar with the matter.

item.title: What happened when one university set out to purge ‘woke’ classes
item.description: The University of Florida and other red-state schools are fostering a conservative vision for the humanities to compete against courses with more diverse perspectives.

item.title: What is really breaking America? Two drinking fountains for $375,000.
item.description: Stop the Machine. Measure success by results delivered, not tax dollars spent.

item.title: A warning in today’s On the Record
item.description: A warning in today’s On the Record

item.title: Why some Republicans are worried about Ken Paxton as a Senate nominee
item.description: Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton won the Republican Senate primary runoff in his state Tuesday night, ousting incumbent Sen. John Cornyn. Paxton has ignited the MAGA base, but few politicians have garnered as much scandal in the state.

item.title: Texas runoffs. Unpopular parties. Federal workers. College classes. Summer forecast. World Cup. Reflecting Pool. Catch up now.
item.description: Catch up in minutes with these 7 stories.

item.title: Democrat Johnny Garcia wins nomination for key House seat in Texas, defeating opponent who called for imprisoning Zionists
item.description: Party leaders had denounced Maureen Galindo, a sex therapist with little political experience, for remarks she made espousing castrating and imprisoning “American Zionists.” Her comments brought new attention to the race in Texas’s 35th Congressional District.

item.title: Sen. John Cornyn loses primary challenge in Republican runoff in Texas, ending a bitter Senate race driven by a MAGA insurgency
item.description: Ken Paxton, the scandal-plagued state attorney general, is the second Trump-endorsed Republican to oust an incumbent senator in less than two weeks and will face Democrat James Talarico in November. The contest ballooned into the most expensive Senate primary in history, with Cornyn’s defeat ending a storied career in Texas and national politics.

item.title: Six things a neurologist does to keep his brain healthy
item.description: Brain atrophy tends to begin in your 30s and 40s, but certain lifestyle changes can slow or even reverse shrinkage.

item.title: The next Darwin moment has arrived
item.description: Like evolution before it, AI may force a rethink of what makes humans special.

item.title: Trump fumes at Congress and courts for holding up his ballroom
item.description: The president bulldozed the East Wing and remade federal panels that quickly approved his ballroom project. But he has faced barriers as he seeks to complete it.

item.title: South Carolina Republicans block Trump’s effort to gain more House seats
item.description: The failure to approve a new map in South Carolina preserved the district of Rep. James E. Clyburn, a power broker within the Democratic Party and the first Black member of Congress elected from the state in nearly a century.

item.title: Doctor evacuated from Congo feels ‘helpless’ watching colleagues die of Ebola
item.description: Surrounded by a plastic bubble, Patrick LaRochelle spoke to The Post as he waits in a specialized hospital room in Prague to see whether he has Ebola.

item.title: Carolyn Hax: Son can serve his country but can’t acknowledge parents’ birthdays
item.description: Their son still visits but ignores the family’s special days. Is this a sign of some deep-seated resentment?

item.title: Trump administration proposes nondisclosure agreements for all federal workers in effort to crack down on media leaks
item.description: The planned rule would bar federal workers from sharing “confidential government information,” applying an expansive definition of privileged information. The rule would expand NDAs instituted at the Pentagon and other agencies.

item.title: Do Russians support the Ukraine war? This poll is remarkable.
item.description: Repressions mount as Russians turn against the Ukraine conflict.

item.title: Federal court blocks redistricting map created to help Republicans in Alabama
item.description: The court's decision delivers a win to Democrats. The new voting map had been designed to give Republicans an edge in several congressional districts for November's elections. The decision could be appealed.

item.title: A clothing ‘betrayal’ in today’s On the Record
item.description: A clothing ‘betrayal’ in today’s On the Record

item.title: Tehran threatens retaliation after U.S. strikes in southern Iran
item.description: The attacks prompted Iran to warn that it would “leave no act of hostility unanswered,” complicating negotiations to end the war.

item.title: Iran strikes. Texas runoff. Trump’s ballroom rage. AI warning. Ebola doctor. Sleep sweet spot. Enhanced Games. Catch up now.
item.description: Catch up in minutes with these 7 stories.

item.title: U.S. renews strikes on Iran, citing threats posed to American troops
item.description: The military said the strikes were conducted in southern Iran “to protect our troops from threats posed by Iranian forces.” Targets included missile-launch sites and Iranian boats attempting to set mines, the military said in a statement. The strikes occurred after a weekend of negotiations between the Trump administration and the Iranian regime to bring the war to a close.
